Beginner.React Native Basics: React Native, created by Meta, allows developers to build mobile applications using JavaScript and React. Unlike hybrid frameworks like Cordova that simply wrap web views, React Native compiles your JavaScript down to native iOS and Android components. This means an application built with React Native has the look, feel, and performance of a native app built in Swift or Kotlin. It relies on a 'Bridge' that communicates asynchronously between the JavaScript thread and the Native OS thread. React Native fundamentally changes mobile development by allowing a single codebase, and a single team of web developers, to deploy true mobile applications across multiple platforms simultaneously.
Beginner.Core Components:
In web development with React, you use DOM elements like `
`, ``, and ``. React Native does not use the DOM; instead, it provides Core Components that map directly to native UI widgets. The most fundamental component is ``, which acts like a `
`, serving as a container for layout. For displaying text, you must use the `` component—you cannot place text directly inside a ``. Other essentials include `` for graphics, `` for scrolling content, and `` for forms. Understanding the mapping from web concepts to these core native components is the first hurdle in transitioning from React web to React Native.
Beginner.Flexbox Layout: React Native uses Flexbox to define the layout of its components. The Flexbox algorithm is identical to CSS Flexbox on the web, with a few crucial default differences to cater to mobile screens: the default `flexDirection` in React Native is `column` (top to bottom), whereas on the web it is `row` (left to right). Layout is achieved by nesting `<View>` components and applying styles like `justifyContent` (alignment along the primary axis) and `alignItems` (alignment along the secondary axis). Mastering Flexbox is absolutely critical because React Native does not support standard CSS positioning models like floats or percentage-based grid systems.
Beginner.State & Props: State and Props are the core mechanisms for managing data in any React application. 'Props' (short for properties) are read-only data passed down from a parent component to a child component, allowing components to be dynamic and reusable. 'State', managed by the `useState` hook, is mutable data internal to a component. When a component's state changes—perhaps due to a user tapping a button or data arriving from an API—React automatically re-renders the component to reflect the new state. Understanding the unidirectional data flow (props go down) and state management is what makes React interfaces declarative and predictable.
Beginner.Expo CLI: Expo is a framework and platform built around React Native that makes development significantly easier. While you can build 'bare' React Native apps, doing so requires managing complex native build tools like Xcode and Android Studio. Expo abstracts this complexity away. It provides an Over-The-Air (OTA) client (Expo Go) that allows you to scan a QR code and instantly run your app on your physical device during development without compiling native code. Expo also provides a vast SDK containing ready-to-use APIs for common mobile features like the camera, push notifications, and location services, making it the recommended starting point for new projects.

Intermediate.Navigation (React Navigation): Mobile apps require navigation between multiple screens. Unlike the web, which has built-in browser history and URLs, React Native requires a dedicated library for this. React Navigation is the community-standard routing solution. It provides different types of navigators: Stack Navigators (which push new screens on top of each other, providing the standard 'back' button behavior), Tab Navigators (the standard bottom bar seen in iOS), and Drawer Navigators (a slide-out side menu). Implementing React Navigation correctly is vital for application structure, ensuring smooth transitions, and passing parameters between different screens in a type-safe manner.
Intermediate.Lists & ScrollViews: Mobile screens are small, making scrolling interfaces ubiquitous. While `<ScrollView>` works for short blocks of content, it is highly inefficient for long lists because it renders all items in the DOM at once, consuming massive memory. React Native provides `<FlatList>` and `<SectionList>` for performant rendering of large datasets. `FlatList` only renders the items currently visible on the screen (plus a small buffer), recycling components as the user scrolls. It requires two main props: `data` (the array of items to render) and `renderItem` (a function describing how to display a single item). Mastering `FlatList` is essential for performance.
Intermediate.Styling (StyleSheet): Styling in React Native is done using JavaScript objects rather than separate CSS files. While inline styles are possible, the standard practice is to use the `StyleSheet.create()` API. This method validates the styles at runtime, improves performance by caching the styles, and keeps the component code cleaner. React Native styling only supports a subset of CSS properties, primarily focused on layout (Flexbox) and basic typography/colors. It does not support media queries, pseudo-classes (like `:hover`), or complex animations directly via styles. Understanding how to organize and share `StyleSheet` objects is crucial for maintaining a consistent design system.
Intermediate.Handling Touches: Interactivity on mobile is driven by touch, not clicks. React Native provides several components to handle user touch events. `<TouchableOpacity>` is the most common; when pressed, it briefly decreases the opacity of the wrapped view, providing immediate visual feedback to the user. `<TouchableHighlight>` provides a background color change on press, common on iOS lists. For customized, complex gestures (like swiping or pinching), developers use the lower-level `PanResponder` API or, more commonly, the highly optimized `react-native-gesture-handler` library. Proper touch feedback is a hallmark of a high-quality, native-feeling mobile application.
Intermediate.Device APIs (Camera, Location): One of the main reasons to build a mobile app instead of a web app is to access native device hardware. Through Expo or native modules, React Native provides APIs to interface with the Camera, GPS, Accelerometer, and File System. Using these APIs requires handling permissions asynchronously, as both iOS and Android enforce strict privacy controls requiring explicit user consent before accessing hardware. Implementing these features involves requesting permission, handling the granted/denied states gracefully in the UI, and then utilizing the specific SDK methods to capture a photo or poll for the current latitude and longitude.

Advanced.Animations (Reanimated): Smooth, 60fps animations are critical for mobile UX. The built-in React Native `Animated` API is powerful but can struggle with complex sequences because it relies on the React Native Bridge to pass animation values between JavaScript and the native thread. `react-native-reanimated` is a highly advanced, community-driven library that solves this. It allows you to declare animations in JavaScript that execute entirely on the native UI thread, bypassing the bridge completely. This ensures animations remain smooth even when the JavaScript thread is busy parsing data or rendering complex components. Mastering Reanimated elevates an app from 'good' to 'premium'.
Advanced.Native Modules Basics: While React Native and Expo cover 95% of use cases, there are times when you need access to a specific native iOS or Android API that hasn't been wrapped in JavaScript, or you need to execute heavy computation in native code (Swift/Kotlin/C++) for performance. In these cases, developers build Native Modules. This involves writing native code for each platform and creating a 'bridge' interface to expose those native functions to your JavaScript environment. This requires dropping out of the managed Expo workflow and requires knowledge of Xcode (iOS) and Android Studio, bridging the gap between JS and raw mobile development.
Advanced.State Management (Redux/Zustand): As applications grow, passing state down through multiple layers of components via props (prop drilling) becomes unmanageable. Global State Management solutions solve this. While Context API works for simple cases, large apps require dedicated libraries. Redux is the traditional heavyweight, enforcing strict unidirectional data flow through Actions, Reducers, and a central Store. Recently, Zustand has become highly popular in the React Native ecosystem for providing a much simpler, less boilerplate-heavy alternative that uses hooks directly. Choosing and implementing the right global state architecture is vital for decoupling data logic from UI rendering in enterprise apps.
Advanced.Offline Storage (AsyncStorage): Mobile apps must be resilient to poor or nonexistent network conditions. `AsyncStorage` is an unencrypted, asynchronous, persistent, key-value storage system that is global to the app. It is similar to `localStorage` on the web but is strictly asynchronous (returning Promises). It is used for persisting user preferences, authentication tokens, or caching small amounts of API data. For more complex, relational local data, developers often turn to SQLite databases (via plugins like `expo-sqlite`) or solutions like WatermelonDB, which are optimized to handle large datasets and synchronize cleanly with remote backends when the network returns.
Advanced.App Deployment & CI/CD: Building the app is only half the battle; deploying it to the Apple App Store and Google Play Store is a complex process. This involves managing cryptographic signing certificates, provisioning profiles, and understanding app store guidelines. Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines automate this. Tools like Fastlane or Expo Application Services (EAS) allow you to define the build and submission processes in code. A proper CI/CD setup can automatically run tests, increment build numbers, compile native binaries in the cloud, and upload them directly to TestFlight or Google Play Console whenever code is merged to the main branch.
